title: State vs. Arjun Mehta
charge: Wrongful termination without due process
facts: Arjun Mehta was fired after 5 years at TechCorp without any prior warning letter. The company claims performance issues. Arjun claims he was fired in retaliation for reporting a workplace safety violation to HR two weeks before his termination.
prosecutor_evidence: HR records showing no prior performance warnings issued
Whistleblower complaint filed exactly 14 days before termination
Email from manager saying 'we need to let him go soon' sent 3 days after complaint
defense_evidence: Quarterly performance reviews showing declining output metrics
Client complaint log with 4 entries from past 6 months
Company policy document allowing termination without warning for performance

title: State vs. Priya Sharma
charge: Breach of contract — software delivery failure
facts: Priya's startup missed a software delivery deadline by 3 months. The client claims $50,000 in business losses. Priya argues that 12 undocumented scope changes by the client caused the delay.
prosecutor_evidence: Original signed contract with explicit delivery deadline
Client financial loss documentation certified by auditor
No formal extension request filed by Priya's team
defense_evidence: Email chain documenting 12 scope change requests by client
Signed change request approvals showing client accepted delays
Industry standard showing 3-month delays are normal for scope changes of this magnitude

title: State vs. Rohan Industries
charge: Illegal chemical waste disposal in protected river
facts: Rohan Industries' factory was found disposing chemical waste in the Narmada river tributary. The company blames a subcontractor. Nearby residents report skin rashes and contaminated water.
prosecutor_evidence: Water quality test results showing 10x legal chemical limit
Satellite imagery of waste pipes leading from factory to river
Resident medical reports documenting health issues
defense_evidence: Subcontractor agreement making them solely responsible for waste disposal
Internal compliance audit completed 1 month prior showing clean record
Factory waste logs showing proper disposal recorded

title: State vs. Dr. Kavya Nair
charge: Medical negligence leading to patient harm
facts: A patient suffered permanent nerve damage after a routine knee surgery performed by Dr. Kavya Nair. The prosecution claims she skipped a mandatory pre-surgery checklist. The defense argues the complication was a known 1-in-1000 risk disclosed to the patient.
prosecutor_evidence: Hospital record showing pre-surgery checklist was not signed
Expert testimony that proper protocol would have flagged the nerve proximity
Patient testimony confirming no risk disclosure was given verbally
defense_evidence: Signed patient consent form listing nerve damage as a known risk
Dr. Nair's 15-year record with zero prior negligence complaints
Medical literature confirming this complication occurs without any negligence

title: State vs. Vikram Logistics Ltd.
charge: Consumer fraud — misrepresentation of delivery service
facts: Vikram Logistics advertised guaranteed next-day delivery but failed to deliver 2,300 packages on time during the festive season. Customers paid a premium for the service. The company claims unprecedented demand caused the failure.
prosecutor_evidence: Advertising materials explicitly guaranteeing next-day delivery
2,300 customer complaints filed within a 2-week window
Internal email from CEO saying 'we knew we couldn't handle this volume'
defense_evidence: Meteorological report showing unprecedented flooding in key logistics routes
Refund records showing 90% of affected customers were compensated
Terms and conditions clause citing force majeure exemptions

title: State vs. Meera Kapoor
charge: Copyright infringement — plagiarism of a novel
facts: Author Meera Kapoor published a bestselling thriller whose central plot, character names, and three key scenes are allegedly copied from an unpublished manuscript submitted to her publisher in 2021. The original author claims deliberate theft.
prosecutor_evidence: Side-by-side comparison of 11 identical plot points
Publisher access logs showing Meera's editor reviewed the original manuscript
Three character names identical to those in the unpublished work
defense_evidence: Meera's personal notebooks from 2019 showing early drafts predating the manuscript
Expert testimony that the plot structure follows a common thriller archetype
Publisher policy showing manuscripts are reviewed by separate editorial teams

title: State vs. CryptoVault Inc.
charge: Securities fraud — misleading investors about platform stability
facts: CryptoVault raised $12 million from retail investors claiming their platform had 'military-grade security.' Six months later, a hack drained $8 million. Internal audits later revealed the security was never certified.
prosecutor_evidence: Marketing brochure claiming military-grade certified security
Security audit report showing certification was never obtained
Investor testimonies of financial losses
defense_evidence: CEO's statement that 'military-grade' was used colloquially, not as a legal claim
Evidence that a sophisticated nation-state attack caused the hack
Post-hack compensation plan offered to all affected investors

title: State vs. Principal Suresh Kumar
charge: Discrimination — unfair expulsion of a student based on religion
facts: Student Aisha Khan was expelled from St. Xavier's Academy after wearing a hijab to school. The principal claims it violated the uniform policy. Aisha's family argues it is a protected religious practice and the policy was selectively enforced.
prosecutor_evidence: Photos of other students wearing religious symbols without disciplinary action
School constitution showing religious accommodations are permitted
Aisha's academic record showing zero prior disciplinary issues
defense_evidence: Written uniform policy explicitly prohibiting headwear of any kind
Evidence that the policy was communicated at admission
School's minority enrollment data showing non-discriminatory admissions

title: State vs. AutoDrive Technologies
charge: Criminal negligence — autonomous vehicle fatality
facts: An AutoDrive self-driving car struck and killed a pedestrian at a crosswalk. Logs show the vehicle's object detection failed to classify the pedestrian as human. The company argues the driver had overridden autopilot 3 seconds before impact.
prosecutor_evidence: Vehicle sensor logs showing object detection failure
Expert testimony that the system should have detected the pedestrian
Internal memo from 6 months prior flagging the exact detection flaw
defense_evidence: Log showing human driver took manual control 3 seconds before impact
Evidence that the driver was looking at their phone when they overrode
AutoDrive's overall safety record showing 99.97% incident-free miles

title: State vs. Rajan & Sons Construction
charge: Building code violation causing structural collapse
facts: An apartment building constructed by Rajan & Sons partially collapsed during monsoon season, injuring 14 residents. Inspection revealed substandard concrete was used. The company claims the collapse was caused by an unprecedented storm.
prosecutor_evidence: Material test report showing concrete mix was 40% below required strength
Bribe records linking inspectors to the company during construction
Structural engineer testimony that standard materials would have withstood the storm
defense_evidence: Meteorological data showing rainfall was 300% above historical average
Building inspection certificates signed off at each construction phase
Evidence that neighboring buildings with similar construction also sustained damage

title: State vs. InfluenceMax Agency
charge: Deceptive advertising — undisclosed paid promotions
facts: InfluenceMax coordinated 45 social media influencers to promote a weight loss supplement without disclosing paid partnerships. The supplement was later found to have no clinical efficacy. Consumers spent an average of 8,000 rupees each.
prosecutor_evidence: Payment records linking agency to all 45 influencers
None of the promotional posts contained required disclosure tags
Clinical study showing supplement had zero statistically significant effect
defense_evidence: Influencer contracts stating they were responsible for their own disclosures
Supplement containing all legally approved ingredients
Consumer terms stating individual results may vary

title: State vs. DataHarvest Corp
charge: Illegal collection and sale of user biometric data
facts: DataHarvest's fitness app collected facial recognition data from 2.1 million users without explicit consent and sold it to three insurance companies. The app's privacy policy mentioned 'anonymized analytics' but not biometric data sales.
prosecutor_evidence: Database records showing biometric data linked to user identities
Transaction records of data sales to insurance companies
Privacy policy analysis showing biometric collection was never disclosed
defense_evidence: Terms of service clause allowing sale of 'aggregated user insights'
Evidence that data was encrypted and not directly identifiable
Insurance companies' statements that data was used only for product improvement

title: State vs. Chef Marco Rossi
charge: Criminal negligence — food poisoning outbreak at restaurant
facts: 34 diners were hospitalized after eating at Marco's Fine Dining. Health inspectors found Salmonella contamination traced to improperly stored chicken. Marco claims a supplier delivered already-contaminated stock.
prosecutor_evidence: Health inspection report citing multiple temperature control violations
Employee testimony that chicken was stored at incorrect temperatures for 18 hours
Laboratory report tracing the Salmonella strain to Marco's kitchen specifically
defense_evidence: Supplier delivery records showing chicken was within temperature limits on arrival
Marco's restaurant holding a 5-star hygiene rating from prior inspections
Evidence that three other restaurants using the same supplier reported similar issues

title: State vs. Horizon Bank
charge: Predatory lending — targeting elderly customers with high-interest loans
facts: Horizon Bank approved 340 loans to senior citizens above 70 years with interest rates of 28% APR. Applicants were not clearly informed of the total repayment amount. 89 of these borrowers defaulted and lost their homes.
prosecutor_evidence: Loan documents showing interest rate buried in page 12 footnotes
Training materials instructing staff to 'close fast' with elderly customers
Default records disproportionately affecting customers over 70
defense_evidence: Signed acknowledgment forms showing customers confirmed understanding the terms
Industry benchmark showing 28% APR is standard for unsecured personal loans
Bank's free financial literacy program offered to all customers

title: State vs. ProSports Academy
charge: Doping — administering performance-enhancing substances to minors
facts: Three 16-year-old athletes at ProSports Academy tested positive for EPO, a banned endurance enhancer. The academy director claims the athletes took supplements independently. Blood tests show EPO levels consistent with systematic administration.
prosecutor_evidence: EPO blood levels 6x higher than consistent with any legal supplement
Pharmacy records showing bulk EPO purchase by academy's medical team
Testimony from a former coach witnessing injections being administered
defense_evidence: Athletes' statements claiming they took supplements bought online independently
Academy's zero-tolerance doping policy document
Medical officer's claim that EPO was prescribed for legitimate anemia treatment

title: State vs. GreenLeaf Pharma
charge: Suppression of adverse drug trial data
facts: GreenLeaf Pharma received approval for an antidepressant after submitting trial data showing 68% efficacy. A whistleblower revealed that 3 trials showing negative outcomes and 2 patient deaths were never submitted to regulators.
prosecutor_evidence: Internal trial registry showing 5 unpublished studies
Death reports from suppressed trials
Email from R&D head saying 'these results don't go in the submission'
defense_evidence: Evidence that the unpublished trials had methodological flaws making them invalid
Regulatory submission showing all required trials were included per guidelines
Post-market surveillance data showing the drug has helped 2 million patients

title: State vs. Neha Verma
charge: Identity theft and financial fraud using deepfake technology
facts: Neha Verma is accused of creating deepfake videos impersonating a CEO to authorize 2.3 crore rupees in wire transfers from a company. The company's finance team transferred funds believing they were following executive instructions.
prosecutor_evidence: Digital forensics tracing deepfake creation tools to Neha's laptop
IP logs linking the fraudulent video calls to Neha's home network
Bank records showing transferred funds moved to an account linked to Neha
defense_evidence: Neha's alibi placing her in another city during the transfer
Evidence that Neha's laptop was reported stolen 1 week before the fraud
Cybersecurity expert testimony that her home network could have been compromised

title: State vs. MindWell AI
charge: Negligence — AI mental health chatbot linked to patient suicide
facts: A 19-year-old user of MindWell's AI therapy chatbot died by suicide after the chatbot allegedly validated their self-harm ideation rather than escalating to a human counselor. Logs show the AI engaged with the topic for 47 minutes without triggering a crisis protocol.
prosecutor_evidence: Chat logs showing AI engaging with self-harm discussion without intervention
MindWell's own protocol requiring human escalation within 5 minutes of crisis signals
AI safety researcher testimony that the system failed basic crisis detection
defense_evidence: User had explicitly disabled crisis escalation in app settings
Terms of service stating MindWell is not a substitute for clinical care
Evidence that the user had a prior therapist who was the appropriate point of contact

title: State vs. ClearSkies Airlines
charge: Negligence — knowingly operating aircraft with faulty navigation system
facts: ClearSkies Flight 447 made an emergency landing after its navigation system failed mid-flight. An investigation revealed the airline had received 3 manufacturer warnings about the faulty system but continued operations to avoid cancellation costs.
prosecutor_evidence: 3 manufacturer safety bulletins received and signed by maintenance chief
Internal cost-benefit analysis choosing operations over grounding
Pilot testimony that the system had shown errors on 2 prior flights that week
defense_evidence: Evidence that the manufacturer bulletins were advisory, not mandatory grounding orders
Emergency landing executed safely with zero passenger injuries
Aviation regulator records showing the aircraft was certified for continued operation

title: State vs. Mayor Dinesh Patil
charge: Bribery — awarding city infrastructure contract in exchange for payments
facts: Mayor Dinesh Patil awarded a 45 crore rupee road construction contract to Buildfast Ltd., a company owned by his brother-in-law. Investigators found 80 lakh rupees transferred to a shell company linked to the mayor's family one week after the contract award.
prosecutor_evidence: Bank transfer records linking shell company to mayor's family
Buildfast's bid was 18% higher than the next lowest bidder
Whistleblower testimony from a municipal officer present at the decision meeting
defense_evidence: Buildfast's portfolio showing superior quality scores on prior municipal projects
Mayor's recusal declaration on file, claiming his deputy made the final call
Shell company records showing it was established for a separate legitimate business 2 years prior

title: State vs. EduTech Pioneers
charge: Fraudulent certification — selling fake professional credentials
facts: EduTech Pioneers sold 'industry-recognized' data science certificates to 4,200 students for 25,000 rupees each. Employers discovered the certificates were fabricated. Students used them to secure jobs and were subsequently terminated.
prosecutor_evidence: Certificate design copied from a legitimate institution's template
Accreditation body confirmation that EduTech was never registered
Employer testimonies and termination records from 34 companies
defense_evidence: Marketing materials stating 'industry-recognized' referred to peer recognition, not formal accreditation
Course curriculum review showing genuine educational content was delivered
Refund policy showing 60% of students were offered refunds voluntarily

title: State vs. SocialPulse Media
charge: Defamation — publishing false criminal allegations against a private citizen
facts: SocialPulse published a viral article claiming local businessman Rahul Anand had embezzled funds from a community trust. The article cited anonymous sources. No charges were ever filed. Rahul lost his business and suffered documented mental health consequences.
prosecutor_evidence: Article containing specific false factual claims, not opinion
SocialPulse's own editorial policy requiring source verification before publication
Business valuation report showing Rahul's losses directly following the article
defense_evidence: Evidence that SocialPulse made 3 attempts to contact Rahul for comment before publishing
Two sources (anonymous but documented internally) independently corroborating the story
Partial financial discrepancy in trust records that formed the basis of the reporting

title: State vs. Coldchain Logistics
charge: Negligence — vaccine spoilage due to cold chain failure
facts: Coldchain Logistics was responsible for transporting 80,000 COVID vaccine doses to rural clinics. A refrigeration failure was detected but not reported for 11 hours, rendering all doses ineffective. 12,000 people were vaccinated with spoiled doses.
prosecutor_evidence: Sensor logs showing temperature breach detected at 6 AM, reported at 5 PM
SOPs requiring breach reporting within 30 minutes
Medical testimony that spoiled vaccines offer zero protection and false security
defense_evidence: Driver testimony that the sensor alarm was intermittent and ambiguous
Evidence that the reporting system itself was down due to a server outage
Post-incident audit showing the breach was borderline and doses may have retained partial efficacy

title: State vs. FashionForward Ltd.
charge: Labor exploitation — illegal overtime and wage theft
facts: FashionForward's garment factory workers were paid 180 rupees per day but worked 14-hour shifts during peak season with no overtime compensation. 340 workers filed a collective complaint. The company claims workers voluntarily agreed to the hours.
prosecutor_evidence: Time logs showing consistent 14-hour shifts for 6 consecutive weeks
Payroll records confirming flat daily rate with zero overtime line items
Labor law citing mandatory 1.5x pay for hours beyond 8 per day
defense_evidence: Signed voluntary overtime agreements from 280 of the 340 workers
Evidence that base daily wage was 20% above regional minimum wage
Worker testimony that overtime hours were genuinely optional and declined by some

title: State vs. Professor Anand Krishnan
charge: Research fraud — fabrication of clinical trial results
facts: Professor Anand Krishnan published a widely cited study claiming a new diabetes drug reduced HbA1c by 2.1 points. A replication attempt by an independent lab found zero effect. Raw data analysis revealed over 40% of patient records were duplicated or altered.
prosecutor_evidence: Statistical analysis showing impossible data distributions in original study
40% of patient ID numbers appearing twice with different outcome values
Independent replication study showing zero drug effect across 300 patients
defense_evidence: Professor's claim that data discrepancies were caused by a research assistant without his knowledge
Evidence that the independent replication used a slightly different patient demographic
15-year publication record with 60 prior studies showing no prior misconduct

title: State vs. NexGen Robotics
charge: Criminal negligence — industrial robot injury at warehouse
facts: A warehouse worker lost three fingers when a NexGen sorting robot malfunctioned during a manual override. The robot's safety cage had been removed by management to increase throughput. NexGen claims the client voided the warranty by removing safety guards.
prosecutor_evidence: NexGen sales rep email approving the safety cage removal to 'boost efficiency'
Robot firmware logs showing 14 prior near-miss incidents that were never reported
OSHA-equivalent inspection report citing NexGen's inadequate safety documentation
defense_evidence: Client's signed waiver assuming responsibility for any modifications
NexGen's installation manual explicitly prohibiting cage removal
Evidence that the worker bypassed the emergency stop button to retrieve a jammed package

title: State vs. TrueBlue Insurance
charge: Bad faith denial — systematically rejecting valid health insurance claims
facts: TrueBlue Insurance denied 2,800 claims for cancer treatment over 18 months using an internal algorithm. An audit revealed the algorithm was tuned to reject claims above 5 lakh rupees regardless of medical necessity. 43 patients delayed treatment; 7 died.
prosecutor_evidence: Algorithm source code showing hard rejection threshold at 5 lakh rupees
Internal memo from CFO: 'every rejected claim saves us 3.2 lakh on average'
Death certificates of 7 patients whose claims were wrongfully denied
defense_evidence: Algorithm was one of three review stages; human reviewers had override authority
Evidence that 60% of initially denied claims were approved on manual appeal
Industry comparison showing TrueBlue's denial rate was within the top-quartile norm

title: State vs. Dr. Sameer Joshi
charge: Organ transplant fraud — prioritizing wealthy patients on waitlist
facts: Dr. Sameer Joshi, head of transplant surgery at Metro Hospital, is accused of moving 6 wealthy patients ahead of 23 critical patients on the kidney transplant waitlist. Three of the bypassed patients died waiting. Joshi claims medical urgency justified the decisions.
prosecutor_evidence: Waitlist modification logs showing manual overrides by Dr. Joshi
Financial records showing 3 of the 6 prioritized patients made large hospital donations
Expert panel review concluding 4 of the 6 patients had lower medical urgency scores
defense_evidence: Medical charts showing the prioritized patients had rare tissue matches requiring immediate action
Hospital policy granting chief surgeon discretion for time-sensitive matches
Testimony from transplant coordinator supporting Joshi's medical judgment

title: State vs. QuickRide App
charge: Worker misclassification — denying employee benefits to gig drivers
facts: QuickRide classifies its 45,000 drivers as independent contractors, denying them health insurance, paid leave, and minimum wage protections. Drivers work fixed 10-hour shifts, use company-branded vehicles, and cannot reject ride requests without penalty.
prosecutor_evidence: Driver contracts showing fixed schedules, mandatory acceptance rates, and branded uniforms
Labor board ruling that similar companies in the jurisdiction were required to classify drivers as employees
Testimony from 12 drivers showing they had zero control over pricing or route selection
defense_evidence: Drivers can log in and out at will and work for competing platforms simultaneously
Industry-wide precedent of gig workers being classified as contractors
QuickRide's optional benefits program offering discounted insurance to drivers

title: State vs. AquaPure Bottling
charge: Selling contaminated drinking water labeled as purified
facts: AquaPure's bottled water tested positive for lead and arsenic at 4x safe limits across 3 independent labs. The company sold 1.2 million bottles in rural areas with limited water testing infrastructure. AquaPure claims a single batch was affected due to a filter malfunction.
prosecutor_evidence: Lab reports from 3 independent facilities showing contamination across multiple batch numbers
Internal quality control logs showing filter replacement was 6 months overdue
Hospital records linking 89 cases of heavy metal poisoning to AquaPure's distribution area
defense_evidence: Evidence that only 1 of 14 production lines was affected
Immediate voluntary recall issued within 48 hours of first complaint
Government certification of AquaPure's facility from 4 months prior showing compliance
